title: Deployment
description: A serving system practice that turns a trained model plus its runtime stack into a live, versioned inference service on real hardware.
openingSummary: Deployment is the step where a trained model stops being only a checkpoint and becomes a service other systems can actually call. The job is not just to copy weights onto a machine. The job is to fit the model, its KV cache, its batching rules, and its rollback plan into a runtime that stays reliable under real traffic.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: Deployment means putting a model-serving stack into real use. The stack usually includes the weights, tokenizer, runtime, memory plan, request routing, monitoring, and release policy needed to answer inference requests safely.

whereItSits.title: Where It Sits
whereItSits.body: It sits after training and after architecture design. Training changes the weights. Architecture design decides what kind of model exists. Deployment decides whether that finished model can run acceptably on the target hardware, with the target latency, memory budget, and rollback rules.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.body: Teams package a model with a serving runtime, check that the model and its live state fit the chosen hardware, then expose the system behind a controlled rollout. Quantization, batching, and KV-cache strategy matter here because they change whether the service can hold enough concurrent work without running out of memory or missing latency goals.

practicalImpact.title: Practical Impact
practicalImpact.body: Deployment is where abstract serving ideas meet operational reality. A model that looked fine in isolation can fail in production if memory is too tight, if rollout reaches too many users at once, or if rollback is too slow after a bad release.

systemFlow.alt: A deployment flow that packages the model, checks hardware fit, rolls traffic forward, and preserves a rollback path.
systemFlow.caption: Deployment is a controlled serving transition, not a one-time file copy.
